worth_it_explanation: Creative blocking decides which advertisers and ad categories may run on a publisher's video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory, so it sets the line between protecting the brand and the content owner relationship on one side and keeping the inventory filled on the other. A block list that no longer matches the publisher's standards, or one widened so far that it suppresses demand it never needed to, both cost the publisher without anyone reading the list to find out. The check is a review of the block configuration already in SpringServe, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete list the publisher can adjust.
intro: Creative blocking in SpringServe controls which advertiser domains and ad categories are allowed to serve on a publisher's video and CTV inventory. This subcategory is about the block configuration itself, the advertiser-domain and category lists in place and how broadly they are drawn, rather than the demand routing that supplies the ads. These checks confirm that the block lists reflect the publisher's brand standards and any content-owner requirements, and that the blocks are scoped so they protect the inventory without suppressing more demand than the standards call for.

audit_checks.title: Advertiser-domain and category block lists do not match the publisher's standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Block lists that drift from the publisher's brand standards or a content owner's contractual requirements let ads serve that should have been blocked, which puts the brand and any distribution relationship at risk. On CTV inventory, where a channel owner's content licenses often carry explicit category restrictions, a stale list can place a blocked category against premium content and breach the agreement.

educate.base:
A video or Connected TV (CTV) ad server such as SpringServe gives a publisher two main levers for deciding which ads may run on its inventory. An advertiser-domain block list names specific advertiser domains the publisher will not accept, and a category block list names ad categories, such as gambling, alcohol, or political advertising, that the publisher will not serve. Together these define the brand standards the inventory is meant to hold, and the audit question is whether the lists in place still match those standards.

The standards a block list is meant to express come from more than one place. The publisher has its own brand position on what may run against its content. A CTV channel owner or content licensor often adds explicit restrictions through a distribution agreement, for example barring competitor advertising or categories that conflict with the content's rating. A regulator may bar a category outright for certain audiences. When the block lists drift from any of these, the gap shows up as ads that serve when they should not, which is the outcome the standards existed to prevent.

Categorization depends on a shared vocabulary so that a category named on the block list matches the way the incoming ad is described. The IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy and Ad Product Taxonomy give the industry standard category systems for describing content and the advertised product, and a block expressed against those taxonomies is easier to keep consistent than one expressed in ad hoc labels. A block list that names categories the publisher's demand sources do not recognize cannot enforce the standard it was meant to enforce.

A few signs point to block lists that no longer match the publisher's standards:
  • The advertiser-domain or category block list has not been reviewed against the current brand standards or content-owner agreements within a defined cycle
  • A CTV distribution agreement names category restrictions that do not appear on the SpringServe category block list
  • Reporting shows ads serving from advertisers or categories the publisher considers off-limits
  • Category blocks are expressed in labels that do not map to a recognized taxonomy, so they cannot be matched against incoming demand reliably
You may want to confirm that the block lists in SpringServe reflect the publisher's current brand standards and every content-owner requirement that applies. One approach is to gather the written standards and distribution agreements, then read each block list against them to find any restriction that is missing or any block that no longer applies.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, the content licenses behind a CTV channel often carry category restrictions the network negotiated, such as barring a competing network's advertising or categories that clash with a show's audience. You may want to read each distribution agreement against the SpringServe category block list so the contractual restrictions are reflected. A category that serves against licensed content in breach of the agreement can put the distribution relationship at risk.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property where video sits alongside product pages, the brand standard often bars direct competitor advertising and categories that undercut the commerce relationship, for example a rival retailer's ad running against a product review. You may want to confirm the advertiser-domain block list names the competitors the publisher means to exclude. A missing domain lets a competitor's ad serve against the publisher's own commercial content.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser-domain and category block lists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Gather the publisher's written brand standards and any content-owner or distribution agreements, then review the advertiser-domain and category block lists in SpringServe against them to find restrictions that are missing or blocks that no longer apply.

springserve_console.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open the advertiser-domain block list and the category block list that apply to the inventory under audit, and export or record their current entries.
Collect the publisher's written brand standards and every content-owner or distribution agreement that names advertiser or category restrictions for this inventory.
Compare each written restriction against the block lists, and record any restriction that is named in the standards or an agreement but missing from the lists.
Note any block-list entry that no longer corresponds to a current standard, which may be a candidate to remove.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report of served creatives broken out by advertiser domain and ad category for the inventory under audit.
Compare the advertisers and categories that served against the publisher's standards and content-owner agreements.
Record any advertiser or category that served while the standards or an agreement say it should have been blocked.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the advertiser-domain and category block lists alongside the publisher's written standards and content-owner agreements, and notes any restriction named in the standards or an agreement that is missing from the lists. Block lists that reflect every current standard and agreement, with no served advertiser or category that the standards bar, pass. A block list missing a restriction the standards or an agreement require, or reporting that shows an off-limits advertiser or category serving, is a fail recorded with the specific gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the block-list entries and the publisher's standards and content-owner agreements above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser-domain and category block lists for {{site_name}} do not match the publisher's current brand standards and content-owner agreements, so ads can serve that the standards were meant to block. On video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory, a category restriction named in a distribution agreement but missing from the block list can place a barred advertiser against licensed content and put the distribution relationship at risk. {{voice_recommend}} gathering the written standards and every distribution agreement, reading each block list against them to find any missing restriction, and expressing category blocks against a recognized taxonomy so they match the way incoming demand is described. After the update, recheck served creatives by advertiser and category to confirm the lists hold the standard.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are drawn so broadly that they suppress demand the standards do not require
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block list widened beyond the publisher's actual standards, for example a whole category blocked to exclude one offending advertiser, removes demand that was acceptable and lowers fill and revenue on the affected inventory. The loss is quiet because the blocked demand never appears in reporting, so an over-broad list can suppress revenue for a long time before anyone reads it against the standard it was meant to enforce.

educate.base:
A block list protects inventory by removing demand, and every entry on the list is demand the publisher will not earn from. That trade is worth making when the entry reflects a real brand standard. It is a loss with no return when the entry is wider than the standard requires. The publisher gives up acceptable demand to exclude something the standard never named.

Over-broad blocking grows in recognizable ways. A publisher blocks a whole ad category to keep out one advertiser that could have been named on the domain list instead, so every other advertiser in that category is lost with it. A block added for a single campaign or a one-time complaint stays on the list after the reason has passed. A list copied from another property carries restrictions that do not fit the inventory it was applied to. In each case the list does more than the standard asks, and the cost is fill rate and revenue on inventory that could have served acceptable ads.

On a video or Connected TV (CTV) ad server such as SpringServe, the precision of the block matters because demand for video and CTV inventory can be thinner than for display, so each blocked source removes a larger share of the available demand. A category block that sweeps in acceptable advertisers can leave premium inventory underfilled, which is the opposite of what the publisher wanted from holding the standard.

A few signs point to block lists that are wider than the standard requires:
  • A whole category is blocked to exclude a small number of advertisers that an advertiser-domain block could have named directly
  • Block-list entries exist with no record of which standard or agreement requires them
  • The same block list is applied across inventory of different types without checking that each restriction fits each type
  • Fill rate on affected inventory sits below comparable inventory, and the block list is a plausible cause
You may want to confirm that each block-list entry traces to a standard the publisher still holds, and that the block is no wider than that standard requires. One approach is to read the list entry by entry, retire any entry whose reason has passed, and replace a category block with an advertiser-domain block where naming the advertisers directly would hold the same standard with less lost demand.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, demand for premium CTV inventory can be thin enough that a single over-broad category block visibly lowers fill on a channel. You may want to read each category block to confirm it reflects a content-license restriction rather than a one-time decision, and replace a broad category block with named advertiser-domain blocks where that would hold the same standard while keeping more demand.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property, blocking an entire category to exclude a few competitors can remove acceptable advertisers whose products complement the publisher's content rather than compete with it. You may want to name the specific competitor domains on the advertiser-domain block list instead of blocking their whole category, so the complementary demand in that category can still serve.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser-domain and category block lists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each entry on the advertiser-domain and category block lists for the reason behind it, and read fill rate on the affected inventory to see whether the blocks are removing more demand than the standards require.

springserve_console.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open the advertiser-domain and category block lists for the inventory under audit and record every entry.
For each entry, record the standard, agreement, or decision that requires it, and flag any entry with no traceable reason.
Identify any category block whose intent was to exclude a small number of advertisers, which an advertiser-domain block could name directly instead.
Note any list that was copied across inventory types without checking that each restriction fits each type.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report of fill rate and revenue for the inventory affected by the block lists, broken out by ad unit or channel.
Compare fill rate on the affected inventory against comparable inventory with lighter blocking to estimate the demand the blocks remove.
Record any case where fill rate sits below comparable inventory and an over-broad block is a plausible cause.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block-list entry with the standard or agreement that requires it and the fill rate on the affected inventory. Block lists where every entry traces to a current standard and is scoped no wider than that standard requires pass. A list carrying entries with no traceable reason, a category block used to exclude advertisers an advertiser-domain block could name, or fill rate below comparable inventory that an over-broad block plausibly explains, is a fail recorded with the entries and inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block-list entry with its reason and the fill rate on the affected inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the block lists for {{site_name}} are drawn wider than the publisher's standards require, so they remove demand that was acceptable and lower fill and revenue on the affected inventory. A category blocked to exclude a few advertisers an advertiser-domain block could have named directly takes every other advertiser in that category with it, and on video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory, where demand can be thin, each over-broad block removes a larger share. {{voice_recommend}} reading the block lists entry by entry, retiring any entry whose reason has passed, and replacing a category block with named advertiser-domain blocks where that holds the same standard with less lost demand. After the change, recheck fill rate on the affected inventory to confirm the blocks no longer suppress acceptable demand.
